title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, June 4
tldr: The story this week: frontier AI got geopoliticized. In 72 hours we got a US executive order asking labs to hand over pre-release models, Microsoft cutting the OpenAI cord with its own foundation models, Anthropic's IPO filing, China consolidating around DeepSeek, and Mythos shipping to NATO. Capability is no longer the differentiator — sovereignty, distribution, and trust are.

Trump's June 2 AI EO flips last week's narrative. The order he canceled on May 25 returned, reframed as a “voluntary” 30-day pre-release review for any “covered frontier model.” Voluntary in name; coercive once your competitors opt in.

Microsoft Build: in-house foundation models. MAI-Thinking-1 (35B active / ~1T sparse MoE, 256K context, AIME 2026 = 94.5%) and MAI-Code-1-Flash (5B, now default-eligible in GitHub Copilot, +16 pts over Haiku 4.5 on SWE-Bench Pro). Trained without OpenAI data — Redmond is hedging.

Anthropic filed a confidential S-1 on June 1 at a reported ~$47B run-rate; Project Glasswing expanded to 150 more orgs in 15+ countries including NATO and ENISA — frontier AI is now state-grade security infrastructure.
DeepSeek's first-ever VC round is closing at ~$7.4B (Tencent, CATL, founder, NetEase, JD) at a $52–59B valuation — China's answer is a single national champion, not a portfolio.

sections.h: What changed today (and over the 72-hour bridge from Monday)

blocks.sub: Trump's AI EO: “voluntary” pre-release review with mandatory implications
blocks.p: On June 2 the White House issued Promoting Advanced AI Innovation and Security — the order Trump pulled on May 25 returned, but redesigned. Treasury + NSA + DHS get a classified benchmarking process to label any model with “advanced cyber capabilities” a covered frontier model. Labs that opt in submit those models for review 30 days before public release and pick “trusted partners” for early access. Explicitly no licensing, no permitting — but the order also stands up an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse run out of Treasury/NSA/CISA.
blocks.why: Tuesday's daily called the US federal picture “unsettled.” One week later, the federal picture is a soft licensing regime in voluntary clothing. Once OpenAI/Anthropic/Google sign on — which they will, because early-access wins them government contracts — every smaller lab faces an implicit choice: opt in or look reckless. This is also the operational counterpart to Anthropic's Glasswing: government wants the same dual-use security access private labs are now selling.
blocks.doIt: If you ship anything that could plausibly be called a “covered frontier model” (any agent with strong code/security capability), start building the audit artifacts now: model card, eval suite, red-team report, training-data provenance — the same package required for the EU AI Act in Aug 2026. Build the artifact once, use it for both jurisdictions.

blocks.sub: Microsoft Build: the hyperscaler that no longer needs OpenAI's models
blocks.p: At Build (June 2), Microsoft's Superintelligence Team shipped MAI-Thinking-1 (its first in-house reasoning model — 35B active params over a ~1T sparse MoE, 256K context, hits 97.0% AIME 2025 and 94.5% AIME 2026, matches Opus 4.6 on SWE-Bench Pro per Microsoft's blind testing) and MAI-Code-1-Flash (5B coding model, already rolling out as a default-eligible option in GitHub Copilot, claimed +16 pts over Claude Haiku 4.5 on SWE-Bench Pro). Both trained from scratch on “commercially licensed” data — no OpenAI distillation.
blocks.why: Last week's weekly flagged the “license, don't merge” pattern as labs route around antitrust. The mirror move is now visible: a hyperscaler building its own foundation stack so it doesn't depend on a single supplier. Three implications: (1) the April Microsoft–OpenAI exclusivity drop wasn't a one-off, it was the starting gun; (2) Copilot is now a model-agnostic product — MAI-Code-1-Flash, Claude, GPT will compete inside the same picker; (3) “built on clean data” is the new enterprise selling point as IP lawsuits accumulate.
blocks.doIt: If you build on top of GitHub Copilot, instrument per-task model attribution in your usage logs now — Microsoft's default routing will start sending small/cheap requests to MAI-Code-1-Flash silently, and you want to catch quality regressions before users do. Re-run your evals when the picker changes.

blocks.sub: Anthropic S-1 + Glasswing → NATO: AI as state infrastructure
blocks.p: On June 1 Anthropic confidentially filed a draft S-1 with the SEC after the $65B / ~$965B Series H, reportedly on a ~$47B revenue run-rate (up from ~$10B annualized a year ago — fast-moving, directional). The same week, Project Glasswing expanded by 150 orgs across 15+ countries — newly named partners include NATO, ENISA, Samsung, SK Hynix, SK Telecom, and Okta — covering power, water, healthcare, comms, and hardware. Cumulative claim: 10,000+ high/critical vulns surfaced.
blocks.why: Two threads close in one move. (1) The IPO is the first major frontier-lab listing — once it prices, Anthropic's compute spend and gross margins become public, which permanently changes how the rest of the industry is benchmarked. (2) Mythos in NATO and ENISA means Anthropic is now part of allied critical-infrastructure defense — the same posture the Trump EO is formalizing. The export-control conversation about frontier AI just got real.
blocks.doIt: Stop treating model choice as a pure cost/quality decision for any product touching critical infrastructure, government, or EU regulators. Add a third axis: provenance and jurisdiction. Document which model powers which feature and where its weights live — your enterprise buyers will ask within 12 months.

blocks.sub: DeepSeek's first VC round: China picks a champion
blocks.p: DeepSeek is closing its first-ever outside round at ~$7.4B (Bloomberg, June 3) at a $52–59B post-money. Lineup: founder Liang ¥20B of his own money, Tencent ¥10B, CATL ¥5B, plus the National AI Industry Investment Fund, NetEase, JD.com.
blocks.why: DeepSeek built V3/R1/V4 famously without outside capital. Taking ¥50B from a state-fund-aligned consortium is a strategic pivot: they need US-restricted compute, and the only way to get it at scale is to plug into the national champions. Compare to the US picture (multiple frontier labs, distributed cap table) vs. China (one open-weight standard-bearer, concentrated state-adjacent capital). The price-pressure thread from Saturday's daily (DeepSeek 100× cheaper output tokens) is now backed by tens of billions in compute funding — expect the price gap to widen, not close.
blocks.doIt: If you've been delaying a DeepSeek V4 evaluation because “the company is fragile,” drop that thesis — capitalization is no longer the risk. The real risk for Western teams using DeepSeek is now jurisdictional (EU/US data flows, the Trump EO's covered-model logic if you fine-tune).

blocks.sub: OpenAI ships GPT-5.5 + Codex on AWS Bedrock (GA)
blocks.p: Also June 2: GPT-5.5, GPT-5.4, and Codex went GA on Amazon Bedrock after an April limited preview. Codex hit 5M+ weekly users during the preview window (from 4M in April, +25%). Bedrock spend can be applied against existing AWS commits.
blocks.why: Operationally significant: enterprises with AWS commits no longer have a procurement excuse for skipping OpenAI. Strategically, this completes the “OpenAI is on every major cloud” picture (Azure, AWS, Oracle), which is exactly the distribution leverage you'd expect right before an IPO. It also recontextualizes Build: Microsoft shipping in-house models the same day OpenAI ships on AWS Bedrock isn't a coincidence — it's both sides publicly de-coupling.

blocks.sub: Learn the “covered frontier model” concept — it's the new regulatory chess piece
blocks.p: The Trump EO doesn't define “covered frontier model” by parameter count or training FLOPs (the Biden EO's lever). It defines it by capability — specifically, advanced cyber capabilities measured by a classified benchmark. That's a meaningful shift: a small, fine-tuned, open-weight model could in principle qualify if it gets too good at finding vulnerabilities. Three things to internalize:
list: Capability-based regulation = post-hoc. You can't tell at training time whether your model will be covered. Build your eval pipeline so you can measure cyber-offensive capability the same way you measure benchmark scores — METASPLOIT-style task suites, CTF eval sets, vulnerability-discovery rates on a held-out corpus.

The “voluntary” framing is the same one that produced HIPAA Safe Harbor and SOC 2. Voluntary frameworks become market floors within ~24 months because customers demand the badge. Plan to be compliant by mid-2027, not “if” it becomes mandatory.

The EU AI Act and this EO will converge. Both require model cards, eval reports, red-team summaries, and training-data documentation. Build the artifact once as a single source of truth (a private model card + JSON eval results), and emit jurisdiction-specific exports from it. You'll save 6 months of duplicative compliance work.

sources: White House (Promoting Advanced AI Innovation and Security EO, Jun 2) · CNBC / JURIST / Lawfare / Ropes & Gray / Crowell & Moring (EO analysis) · Microsoft AI / GitHub Changelog (MAI-Thinking-1; MAI-Code-1-Flash; Build 2026) · CNBC (Microsoft AI models lessen reliance on OpenAI) · Anthropic (confidential S-1; Expanding Project Glasswing) · TechCrunch / NPR / Yahoo Finance (Anthropic IPO) · Cybersecurity Dive / SecurityWeek (Glasswing → NATO, ENISA, Samsung, Okta) · Bloomberg / SCMP / Tech Startups (DeepSeek ¥50B round) · AWS News Blog / OpenAI (GPT-5.5 + Codex GA on Bedrock). Run-rate, valuation, and benchmark figures are directional.
